1: יְהוָה מָלָךְ תָּגֵל הָאָרֶץ יִשְׂמְחוּ אִיִּים רַבִּים (yĕhwāh mālāk tāgêl hāʾāreṣ yiśmĕḥû ʾiyyîm rabbîm, "The LORD reigns; let the earth rejoice! Let the many coastlands be glad!") — the YHWH mālāk formula (Psalms 93:1; Psalms 96:10; Psalms 99:1) — the enthronement proclamation that the divine warrior has ascended the throne. ʾIyyîm rabbîm: the many islands/coastlands (the most remote inhabited places — Isaiah 41:1,5; Psalms 42:4,10,12 — the distant edges of the known world). The joy of the earth and the distant islands is the proper response to knowing who rules.
2: עָנָן וַעֲרָפֶל סְבִיבָיו צֶדֶק וּמִשְׁפָּט מְכוֹן כִּסְאוֹ (ʿānān waʿărāpel sĕbîbāyw ṣedeq ûmishpāṭ mĕkhôn kisʾô, "Clouds and thick darkness surround him; righteousness and justice are the foundation of his throne!") — ʿānān waʿărāpel: cloud and thick darkness — the theophany vocabulary of Sinai (Exodus 19:16; Psalms 20:21; Deuteronomy 4:11; Psalms 5:22-23). God's hiddenness in the storm is not his absence but his overwhelming presence. The paradox of v2: the concealing darkness and the revealed character (ṣedeq ûmishpāṭ) held together — you cannot see him clearly but you can know what he stands on. Mĕkhôn kisʾô: the foundation/base of his throne (Psalms 89:14 — "righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne").
3: אֵשׁ לְפָנָיו תֵּלֵךְ וּתְלַהֵט סָבִיב צָרָיו (ʾêsh lĕpānāyw têlêk ûtĕlahêṭ sābîb ṣārāyw, "Fire goes before him and consumes his enemies all around!") — ʾêsh lĕpānāyw: fire before him — the pillar of fire (Exodus 13:21); the divine fire of judgment (Deuteronomy 4:24: "your God is a consuming fire"); the fire that went before the ark (Psalms 78:14). Lāhaṭ (piel: to blaze fiercely, to scorch — the consuming intensity that leaves nothing). Ṣārāyw: his adversaries (those who are in hostile opposition to the divine king). The fire that guided Israel consumes Israel's — and God's — enemies.
4: הֵאִירוּ בְרָקָיו תֵּבֵל רָאֲתָה וַתָּחֵל הָאָרֶץ (hêʾîrû bĕrāqāyw têbêl rāʾătāh wattāḥel hāʾāreṣ, "His lightnings illuminate the world; the earth sees and shakes with fear!") — bĕrāqāyw: his lightnings (the divine storm-weapons — Psalms 18:14: "he shot his arrows and scattered the enemy, with great bolts of lightning he routed them"). Rāʾătāh wattāḥel: saw and writhed (the earth as a creature overcome with the labor-pain spasm of divine presence — Psalms 77:16-18). The lightning illuminates the whole world (têbêl) but the sight causes the earth to convulse rather than rejoice.
5: הָרִים כַּדּוֹנַג נָמַסּוּ מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה מִלִּפְנֵי אֲדוֹן כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (hārîm kaddônag nāmassû milipenê yĕhwāh milipenê ʾădôn kol-hāʾāreṣ, "The mountains melt like wax before the LORD, before the Lord of all the earth!") — kaddônag nāmassû: melted like wax (Micah 1:4: "the mountains will melt under him and the valleys will split apart"; Nahum 1:5: "the mountains quake and the hills melt"). Wax is the perfect simile: solid and permanent-seeming, yet responsive to even modest heat. ʾĂdôn kol-hāʾāreṣ: Lord of all the earth — the sovereignty title that encompasses every mountain that melts (used also in Joshua 3:11,13 for the ark as the ark of the Lord of all the earth crossing the Jordan).
6: הִגִּידוּ הַשָּׁמַיִם צִדְקוֹ וְרָאוּ כָל-הָעַמִּים כְּבוֹדוֹ (higgîdû hashshāmayim ṣidqô wĕrāʾû kol-hāʿammîm kĕbôdô, "The heavens proclaim his righteousness, and all the peoples behold his glory!") — higgîdû hashshāmayim: the heavens tell out (nāgad hiphil: to declare plainly — the same root as Psalms 19:1: "the heavens declare the glory of God"). The sky that was illuminated by the divine lightning (v4) becomes the medium of proclamation. Kĕbôdô: his glory (the kābôd that all the peoples witness — the cosmic theophany making God's character visible to every nation).
7: יֵבֹשׁוּ כָּל-עֹבְדֵי פֶסֶל הַמִּתְהַלְלִים בָּאֱלִילִים (yēbōshû kol-ʿōbĕdê pesel hammithallĕlîm bāʾĕlîlîm, "All who serve carved images are put to shame — those who boast in worthless idols!") — pesel: the carved/graven image (the hand-made idol — Exodus 20:4; Deuteronomy 5:8; Isaiah 44:9-20). Hammithallĕlîm: those who boast (hālal hithpael: to boast in, to pride oneself on — the reflexive of praise turned inward toward the idol). Bāʾĕlîlîm: in the worthless nothings (Psalms 96:5 — the ʾĕlîlîm, the nothings). הִשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ כָּל-אֱלֹהִים (hishtaḥăwû-lô kol-ʾĕlōhîm, "all gods bow down before him!") — even the spiritual powers behind the idols prostrate themselves before YHWH (Philippians 2:10-11).
8: שָׁמְעָה וַתִּשְׂמַח צִיּוֹן וַתָּגֵלְנָה בְּנוֹת יְהוּדָה לְמַעַן מִשְׁפָּטֶיךָ יְהוָה (shāmĕʿāh wattismah ṣiyyôn wattāgêlnāh bĕnôt yĕhûdāh lĕmaʿan mishpāṭêkā yĕhwāh, "Zion heard and was glad, and the daughters of Judah rejoiced because of your judgments, O LORD!") — where all the earth trembled and the mountains melted (vv4-5), Zion heard and was glad. The same theophany that terrifies the nations is good news for the covenant people. Bĕnôt yĕhûdāh: the daughters/towns of Judah (the outlying settlements that rejoiced in the capital's God — a merismus for all Judah). Mishpāṭêkā: your judgments — the divine decisions that vindicate the righteous.
9: כִּי-אַתָּה יְהוָה עֶלְיוֹן עַל-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ מְאֹד נַעֲלֵיתָ עַל-כָּל-אֱלֹהִים (kî-ʾattāh yĕhwāh ʿelyôn ʿal-kol-hāʾāreṣ mĕʾōd naʿălêtā ʿal-kol-ʾĕlōhîm, "For you, LORD, are the Most High over all the earth; you are exalted far above all gods!") — ʿelyôn: Most High (the divine epithet of Genesis 14:18-22; Psalms 82:6; Psalms 83:18; Daniel 7:18). Naʿălêtā mĕʾōd: exceedingly exalted (the hithpael/niphal of ʿālāh: to go up — but here in its superlative form: raised up to a degree that exceeds all comparison). This verse grounds the psalm's universal joy (v1) and universal judgment (vv6-7): because YHWH is highest above all, his reign brings appropriate joy to his people and shame to idol-worshippers.
10: אֹהֲבֵי יְהוָה שִׂנְאוּ-רָע שֹׁמֵר נַפְשׁוֹת חֲסִידָיו מִיַּד רְשָׁעִים יַצִּילֵם (ʾōhăbê yĕhwāh sinʾû-rāʿ shōmêr napshôt ḥăsîdāyw miyyad rĕshāʿîm yaṣṣîlêm, "You who love the LORD, hate evil! He guards the lives of his faithful and rescues them from the power of the wicked!") — ʾōhăbê yĕhwāh sinʾû-rāʿ: "those who love the LORD, hate evil!" — the moral corollary of divine love: you cannot love good without hating its opposite (Psalms 45:7; Proverbs 8:13; Amos 5:15; Romans 12:9). Shōmêr napshôt ḥăsîdāyw: he guards the lives/souls of his faithful ones (the ḥăsîdîm — those in covenant loyalty with God). The protective love of God for his faithful is the motivation for the ethical command.
11: אוֹר זָרֻעַ לַצַּדִּיק וּלְיִשְׁרֵי-לֵב שִׂמְחָה (ʾôr zārûaʿ laṣṣaddîq ûlĕyishĕrê-lêb śimḥāh, "Light is sown for the righteous and gladness for the upright in heart!") — ʾôr zārûaʿ: "light sown" — the agricultural metaphor is striking: light is planted like seed in the ground (even though you cannot see it yet, it will sprout). The righteous person's light is not fully visible now — it is buried like a seed in the eschatological soil. Yishĕrê-lêb: the upright in heart (those whose inner orientation is straight, not crooked). Gladness (śimḥāh) planted for them — both terms in the future waiting to emerge.
12: שִׂמְחוּ צַדִּיקִים בַּיהוָה וְהוֹדוּ לְזֵכֶר קָדְשׁוֹ (śimḥû ṣaddîqîm bayhwāh wĕhôdû lĕzêker qōdshô, "Rejoice in the LORD, you righteous, and give thanks to his holy name!") — the psalm closes by circling back to its opening joy (v1: the earth rejoicing), but now addressing the covenant community directly. Śimḥû bayhwāh: rejoice in the LORD (not merely at benefits, but in the divine person himself — Philippians 4:4: "rejoice in the Lord always"). Hôdû lĕzêker qōdshô: give thanks at the remembrance of his holiness — zêker: the memorial, the name-that-is-remembered (Exodus 3:15). Gratitude for the holy character of God is the ultimate form of thanksgiving.
